refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two concerns: (1) the functional requirement to enable teleoperation communication, and (2) a performance constraint on latency. The refined FR isolates the core functional obligation (enable teleoperation) while deferring the quantitative performance criterion to acceptance criteria or design constraints. This keeps the FR solution-neutral and problem-focused: the system must support remote operation capability; how fast that communication must be is a performance attribute, not a functional obligation. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' is vague and belongs in acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms), not in the FR itself.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the teleoperation subsystem can transmit operator commands and sensor feedback between remote operator station and on-site equipment with measurable end-to-end latency. Measure latency under agreed network conditions and confirm it meets the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms). Conduct safety validation with Safety Manager and Tele-operator to confirm latency supports safe operation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator commands from remote operator station to on-site equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit sensor feedback and equipment state from on-site equipment to remote operator station.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication availability under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, using priority-queued message handling and bounded processing windows to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms.

DP2: Deploy a dual-channel redundant communication architecture (primary + failover) with independent latency-optimized paths and automatic switchover logic to ensure safe operation under network degradation.

DP3: Establish a hierarchical control model with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of uplink latency) and supervisory teleoperation commands from the operator, decoupling safety from communication latency.

DP4: Use adaptive bitrate encoding and selective data transmission (prioritize critical control signals over non-essential telemetry) to minimize payload size and transmission latency under variable network conditions.

DP5: Implement a time-synchronized, predictive state machine at the remote site that executes pre-authorized motion sequences with operator override capability, reducing real-time dependency on round-trip latency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish a hierarchical control model with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of uplink latency) and supervisory teleoperation commands from the operator, decoupling safety from communication latency.

potential_sources: DP3 assumes clear separation of safety-critical logic (local, deterministic) from supervisory control (remote, latency-tolerant). Risk: if safety rules and operator commands are not cleanly partitioned in the IM layer, safety logic may inadvertently depend on operator input timing.

DP3 requires reliable state synchronization between operator console and remote site. Risk: if synchronization mechanism is not independently validated, latency spikes could cause operator perception drift.
DP3 couples to the worksite safety plan and operational policies (constraint). Risk: if policies are ambiguous or change, the local enforcement rules may become inconsistent with intent.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is preferred because it directly addresses the root cause: decoupling safety from latency. It is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific protocols or hardware) and supports independent module boundaries (local safety controller vs. remote operator interface). DP1 and DP2 are complementary and should be combined with DP3 at the IM layer. DP4 and DP5 are optimization tactics, not primary architectural solutions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: The worksite safety plan clearly defines which operations are safety-critical (must be enforced locally) and which are supervisory (can tolerate 150 ms latency). Risk: If the safety plan is vague or incomplete, the boundary between local and remote control will be ambiguous, leading to either over-conservative (unusable) or under-safe (dangerous) behavior.

Assumption: The remote site has sufficient computational and sensing capability to enforce safety rules autonomously (e.g., obstacle detection, force limits, position bounds). Risk: If the remote site lacks these capabilities, safety enforcement must remain centralized at the operator, negating the latency decoupling benefit.

Assumption: Operator training and procedures ensure that supervisory commands are issued only when the remote site is in a safe state. Risk: If operators can issue conflicting or unsafe commands, local enforcement alone may not prevent accidents.

Assumption: Network conditions are predictable and bounded (as stated in acceptance criteria). Risk: If network conditions degrade beyond the agreed envelope (e.g., packet loss > threshold, jitter > budget), the p95 ≤ 150 ms target may not be achievable, and fallback procedures must be defined.

Assumption: The IM layer can cleanly separate local safety controller, remote operator interface, and communication middleware. Risk: If these modules are tightly coupled (e.g., safety logic embedded in communication protocol), changes to one will ripple to others, increasing maintenance cost and defect risk.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation, achieved through a hierarchical control architecture in which safety-critical operations are enforced autonomously at the remote site (independent of uplink latency) and supervisory operator commands are transmitted with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions. The solution shall define the boundary between safety-critical (local) and supervisory (remote) operations in accordance with the worksite safety plan and operational policies, and shall include fallback procedures for network degradation beyond the agreed envelope.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hierarchical control logic with local autonomous safety enforcement, independent safety state machines, hazard detection, and safety decision-making at the remote site. Decouples safety policy enforcement from communication latency by maintaining local safety authority.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data and vehicle state from Vehicle module to inform local safety decisions and hazard detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives supervisory teleoperation commands from Teleoperation module; Safety Services validates and gates commands against local safety constraints before forwarding to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits safety violations, hazard alerts, and autonomous safety actions back to operator and Teleoperation module for situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Monitors uplink latency and connection status to inform safety enforcement strategy (e.g., failsafe thresholds); does not block local safety decisions on latency alone.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural home for hierarchical safety control, autonomous enforcement, and latency-independent safety logic. Local safety state machines, hazard detection, and policy enforcement are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to Telemetry (input), Tasking (input gating), SafetyAlert (output), and Network Link (status monitoring). No safety logic leaks into Vehicle or Teleoperation modules.

Poor fit. Vehicle module is responsible for actuation and low-level control, not supervisory safety policy. Placing hierarchical control and autonomous safety enforcement in Vehicle would blur the boundary between vehicle dynamics and safety governance. Safety logic would diffuse across Teleoperation (command validation), Telemetry (state monitoring), and SafetyAlert (alerting). Vehicle should remain a controlled actuator, not a safety arbiter.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is responsible for operator interface and command transmission, not autonomous safety enforcement. Placing hierarchical safety control here would couple safety policy to operator interaction, making it difficult to maintain safety independence from communication latency. Safety logic would diffuse into Vehicle (actuation gating), Telemetry (state monitoring), and SafetyAlert (alerting). Teleoperation should remain a command conduit, not a safety enforcer.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a specific functional module. Placing hierarchical safety control at this level would lack clear ownership and would diffuse safety responsibilities across all subordinate modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). This violates the principle of localized safety authority and makes safety enforcement accountability unclear.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Safety Services module owner (e.g., Safety Engineering team or Autonomous Safety Systems team)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 (hierarchical control with local autonomous safety enforcement independent of uplink latency) is best contained within Safety Services because: (1) Safety Services owns the safety policy domain and is responsible for autonomous hazard response; (2) Local safety state machines and enforcement logic are core to Safety Services' charter; (3) Decoupling safety from communication latency is a safety-domain concern, not a vehicle dynamics or teleoperation concern; (4) Safety Services can receive Telemetry and Tasking inputs, validate commands against safety constraints, and emit SafetyAlert outputs without diffusing logic into other modules; (5) Network Link monitoring informs safety thresholds but does not compromise local safety autonomy.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services does not have direct access to real-time Telemetry, latency in state updates could undermine autonomous safety decisions. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface is low-latency and prioritized.

Risk: If Tasking commands arrive faster than Safety Services can validate them, command queuing or buffering may introduce additional latency. Mitigation: Define clear command validation SLAs and ensure Safety Services has sufficient computational headroom.

Risk: If Vehicle module implements its own safety checks (e.g., motor current limits, mechanical stops), there is a risk of dual safety enforcement and unclear authority. Mitigation: Clarify that Vehicle module implements only low-level protective interlocks; Safety Services owns high-level policy.

Risk: If Teleoperation module attempts to override Safety Services decisions, safety authority is compromised. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract: Teleoperation sends commands, Safety Services gates them; no override path exists.
Risk: SafetyAlert output may be delayed by Network Link congestion, reducing operator situational awareness. Mitigation: Prioritize SafetyAlert traffic and consider redundant alert channels.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Safety Services module boundary is well-defined with clear input/output interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Autonomous safety enforcement logic can be unit-tested independently of communication latency. Latency-independent safety decisions are verifiable through state machine validation and hazard detection test cases. Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms teleoperation latency) is measurable at the Teleoperation module boundary; Safety Services' local enforcement is independent of this latency and can be validated separately.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services is the clear owner of DP3 (hierarchical control with local autonomous safety enforcement). Ownership boundary is unambiguous: Safety Services owns safety policy, state machines, and autonomous hazard response. Vehicle module remains a controlled actuator; Teleoperation remains a command conduit. No competing ownership claims. Interface contracts are explicit: Teleoperation sends commands → Safety Services validates → Vehicle executes. SafetyAlert flows back to operator independently of command latency.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Telemetry latency could undermine real-time hazard detection if sensor data is stale. Mitigation required: Establish Telemetry interface SLA (e.g., ≤50 ms end-to-end from sensor to Safety Services) to ensure autonomous safety decisions are based on current state.

Risk: If Vehicle module implements redundant safety checks (e.g., motor current limits, mechanical stops) without clear coordination with Safety Services, dual enforcement creates ambiguity in safety authority and potential race conditions. Mitigation: Document Vehicle module as implementing only low-level protective interlocks (not policy); Safety Services owns high-level safety policy.

Risk: SafetyAlert output may be delayed by Network Link congestion, reducing operator situational awareness during critical events. Mitigation: Prioritize SafetyAlert traffic with QoS guarantees or consider out-of-band alert channels (e.g., dedicated safety radio).

Risk: If Tasking command validation in Safety Services becomes a bottleneck, command queuing could introduce latency that violates the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion at the system level. Mitigation: Define command validation SLA (e.g., ≤20 ms per command) and ensure Safety Services has sufficient computational headroom.

Risk: Network Link monitoring interface could be misinterpreted as allowing Safety Services to relax safety constraints based on latency. Mitigation: Clarify that Network Link status informs failsafe thresholds only; local safety decisions are never suspended due to latency alone.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.95 score). DP3 logic is tightly bound to Safety Services with minimal diffusion. Hierarchical control, autonomous enforcement, and latency-independent safety decisions are core to Safety Services' charter. Four external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link in) are well-scoped and do not leak safety logic into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution containers. No safety policy enforcement is scattered across modules. Ownership is clear and accountability is localized.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 (hierarchical control with local autonomous safety enforcement independent of uplink latency) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation). By decoupling safety from communication latency through local autonomous enforcement, DP3 ensures that safety decisions are not blocked or delayed by uplink latency. Supervisory teleoperation commands from the operator are gated by Safety Services, ensuring that operator intent is validated against local safety constraints before execution. This design satisfies the functional requirement: safe remote operation is enabled by maintaining safety authority locally, independent of the p95 ≤ 150 ms teleoperation latency constraint. The hierarchical model (local autonomous + supervisory teleoperation) is logically sound and addresses the core tension between operator control and safety independence.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit Telemetry interface SLA (e.g., ≤50 ms end-to-end latency from sensor to Safety Services) to ensure autonomous safety decisions are based on current vehicle state. Document this in the Safety Services interface specification.

Define command validation SLA for Tasking interface (e.g., ≤20 ms per command) to ensure Safety Services does not become a bottleneck that violates the system-level p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion. Include computational headroom analysis.

Clarify the boundary between Safety Services (high-level policy enforcement) and Vehicle module (low-level protective interlocks). Document that Vehicle implements only passive safety measures (e.g., motor current limits, mechanical stops) and does not implement autonomous policy decisions.

Establish a clear interface contract for Tasking: Teleoperation sends commands, Safety Services validates and gates them, Vehicle executes. Document that no override path exists from Teleoperation to Vehicle that bypasses Safety Services.

Prioritize SafetyAlert traffic with QoS guarantees or implement out-of-band alert channels (e.g., dedicated safety radio) to ensure operator situational awareness is not degraded by Network Link congestion.

Document the role of Network Link monitoring in Safety Services: it informs failsafe thresholds and safety strategy adaptation, but does not suspend or relax local safety decisions based on latency alone. Clarify the failsafe behavior when uplink is lost or degraded.

Add acceptance test cases for Safety Services: (1) Autonomous hazard detection and response independent of Teleoperation latency, (2) Command validation and gating under high-latency conditions, (3) SafetyAlert transmission under network congestion, (4) Failsafe behavior when uplink is lost.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence. The Safety Services module boundary is well-defined, ownership is unambiguous, and the hierarchical control logic directly addresses the functional requirement. However, the gate results surface six critical diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that require explicit sub-level decomposition to operationalize. The risks are not blockers but rather indicate that DP3's realization depends on well-specified sub-requirements for interface SLAs, failsafe behavior, and safety policy boundaries. Decomposing to the next level will make these dependencies explicit as sub-FRs, assign ownership to specific sub-DPs, and create verifiable acceptance criteria. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs is essential to ensure that the hierarchical control architecture is not undermined by latency, bottlenecks, or policy diffusion at the implementation layer.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Ensure Telemetry interface delivers current vehicle state to Safety Services with latency ≤50 ms end-to-end (sensor to Safety Services input) to enable autonomous safety decisions based on timely state information.

FR3.2: Validate and gate Teleoperation commands through Safety Services with latency ≤20 ms per command to prevent Safety Services from becoming a bottleneck that violates system-level p95 ≤150 ms acceptance criterion.

FR3.3: Enforce clear ownership boundary between Safety Services (high-level safety policy) and Vehicle module (low-level protective interlocks) such that Vehicle implements only passive safety measures and does not execute autonomous policy decisions.

FR3.4: Establish failsafe behavior and safety strategy adaptation rules when Network Link status degrades or uplink is lost, ensuring local autonomous safety enforcement is never suspended based on latency alone.

FR3.5: Transmit SafetyAlert notifications to operator with priority and reliability guarantees (QoS or out-of-band channels) to maintain operator situational awareness during critical safety events independent of Network Link congestion.

FR3.6: Provide explicit interface contract for command flow (Teleoperation → Safety Services → Vehicle) with no bypass paths, ensuring all operator commands are validated against local safety constraints before execution.

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR3, DP3, and IM3 (Safety Services) are sound. Decomposition to sub-level will operationalize the recommended revisions as explicit sub-requirements.

assumptions: Telemetry sensor data can be delivered to Safety Services with ≤50 ms latency; this is a network/hardware assumption that must be validated.
Safety Services has sufficient computational headroom to validate commands in ≤20 ms; this assumes adequate CPU allocation and no resource contention with other Safety Services tasks.

Vehicle module can be configured to implement only passive safety measures (e.g., motor current limits) without autonomous policy logic; this assumes Vehicle module architecture supports clear separation of concerns.
Network Link monitoring is available to Safety Services (e.g., uplink status, latency metrics); this assumes telemetry or heartbeat mechanism exists.
Out-of-band alert channels (e.g., dedicated safety radio) are feasible or QoS guarantees can be negotiated with Network Link provider; this is a system integration assumption.
Operator can respond to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable time windows; this assumes human factors and situational awareness are within design scope.
open_risks: Telemetry latency SLA (≤50 ms) may not be achievable if sensor-to-Safety Services path includes wireless hops or network congestion. Mitigation: Conduct end-to-end latency measurement and establish fallback strategies (e.g., sensor buffering, predictive state estimation).

Command validation bottleneck: If Safety Services receives high-frequency commands (e.g., joystick input at 100 Hz), ≤20 ms SLA may be unachievable. Mitigation: Define command batching, prioritization, or rate-limiting strategy.

Vehicle module may already implement autonomous safety logic (e.g., motor current limits with adaptive thresholds) that conflicts with Safety Services policy. Mitigation: Audit Vehicle module design and establish clear policy ownership.

Network Link monitoring may introduce false positives (e.g., transient latency spikes) that trigger unnecessary failsafe transitions. Mitigation: Define hysteresis and dwell time for failsafe threshold crossing.

SafetyAlert out-of-band channel (e.g., dedicated radio) may not be available in all deployment scenarios. Mitigation: Define graceful degradation (e.g., best-effort QoS on primary Network Link if out-of-band unavailable).

Operator may not receive or act on SafetyAlert in time to prevent hazard escalation. Mitigation: Ensure autonomous safety enforcement is not dependent on operator response; SafetyAlert is situational awareness only.
evidence_needed: End-to-end latency measurement from sensor to Safety Services input under nominal and degraded network conditions (target: ≤50 ms p95).
Command validation latency profile under various command frequencies and Safety Services load conditions (target: ≤20 ms p95 per command).
Vehicle module design audit: confirm that low-level protective interlocks (motor current limits, mechanical stops) are passive and do not implement autonomous policy decisions.
Network Link monitoring interface specification: define uplink status signals, latency metrics, and failsafe threshold crossing logic.
SafetyAlert transmission test: confirm QoS guarantees or out-of-band channel availability and latency under network congestion scenarios.
Failsafe behavior specification: document safety strategy when uplink is lost, degraded, or recovered; confirm that local autonomous enforcement is never suspended.

Acceptance test cases for Safety Services: (1) autonomous hazard detection and response independent of Teleoperation latency, (2) command validation and gating under high-latency conditions, (3) SafetyAlert transmission under network congestion, (4) failsafe behavior when uplink is lost or recovered.
